chunks.text: Fundamental Conductance Ã· Voltage Limit in Low Voltage Tunnel Switches | EECS at UC Berkeley Fundamental Conductance Ã· Voltage Limit in Low Voltage Tunnel Switches Sapan Agarwal and Eli Yablonovitch EECS Department, University of California, Berkeley Technical Report No. UCB/EECS-2013-247 December 31, 2013 http://www2.eecs.berkeley.edu/Pubs/TechRpts/2013/EECS-2013-247.pdf There is a fundamental conductance Ã· voltage limit in low voltage (less than 4kT/q) tunnel switching devices that obtain a sharp turn off by relying upon the band edges to abruptly cut off the available density of states. The Fermi occupation probabilities are thermally broadened by 4kbT. However, current is only allowed to flow in a narrow energy range limited by the applied voltage, V. This means that if we apply a voltage less than 4kbT/q, the conductance will be reduced by at least qV/4kbT. Even with a perfect tunneling probability of 1 in a perfect quantum channel, the conductance quantum would be diminished by qV/4kT. Attempts at lowering the operating voltage below 4kT/q must come at the expense of smaller conductance.
